title: The Beginning Of The End For GoPro $GPRO

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to the Broken Business
Models channel. I'm your host, James.
And before we get into this video, just
a quick disclaimer that nothing in this
video or on this channel should be taken
as investment advice. I could have
positions in any of the stocks we may
mention in today's video, and I reserve
the right to change my positions at any
time. So, with that out of the way,
let's get into the topic of today's
video, which is GoPro.
So GoPro went public a long time ago,
back in the mid 2010s. And at the time,
back when they went public, they were
viewed as a very successful company.
They basically invented the category of
the action camera. So these, you know,
waterproof cameras that you can take
around when you're in the outdoors to
film what you're doing. And the stock
price was, you know, as high as $80
after their IPO. But then the share
price has lost you know more than well
over 95% of its value and it's currently
a penny stock with you know a share
price of $1.37 and the market cap has
declined to approximately $200 million.
So the company has obviously not done
well over you know a long time horizon.
However, there was a bit of a meme rally
that GoPro experienced in late 2025. Uh
this was not really related to the
company's fundamentals. If you remember
back in, you know, starting around the
summer, but going into the fall of 2025,
there was the stock market was kind of
crazy. There was a lot of meme rally
where you know random companies like
Dunkin Donuts uh Kohl's and GoPro and
Openoor some of these companies which
are actually not the fundamentally these
companies were not doing very well but
they gained the attention of kind of the
Wall Street bets retail investor types
and the share prices increased massively
just based on a meme rally and GoPro was
kind of caught up in that
but as I said earlier the fundamentals
of the business have not done Well, over
the past few years and the main reason
is because in recent years there has
been increasing competition in the
action camera market from mostly Chinese
competitors. So the two biggest
competitors to GoPro are DJ uh DJI and
Insta 360. So DJI is mostly known for
making consumer drones but they also
make action cameras uh which are
considered to be very good. Also, Insta
360 makes action cameras, both DJI and
Insta 360, at least based on some
reviews and metrics, are actually
superior to the GoPro ones uh and are
sold at competitive prices. So, GoPro
has lost significant market share to uh
these Chinese competitors.
Not only that, but as cell phones, you
know, every year there's a new iPhone
and new Samsung and whatever phones,
every year there's a new flagship phone
that has a better and better camera. So,
as people buy these new smartphones with
extremely good cameras, then there's
less of a need to buy to spend a few
hundred dollars extra to buy a dedicated
action camera for so for both those two
re reasons, increased Chinese
competition plus competition from better
phone cameras. Um, GoPro has seen its
market share and revenue decline. And we
can see that just looking over the past
couple years. So, typically the third
quarter is GoPros's seasonally strongest
quarter. And the reason why that is,
well, the holiday season with Christmas
and everything is in the fourth quarter.
And that's when a lot of people buy
GoPros to give as Christmas gifts.
However, GoPro actually recognizes more
revenue in the third quarter because
there's a delay. So, they'll sell the
cameras to retailers. Uh so the
retailers will stock up on GoPro cameras
in the third quarter expecting to sell
them in the fourth quarter. So GoPro
actually recognizes the revenue uh you
know kind of this holiday revenue. They
actually recognize it in the third
quarter because because of the timing of
how they sell it to retailers at
wholesale prices and that's when GoPro
recognizes the revenue. So the third
quarter of 2025, their revenue declined
by 37% compared to the year ago period.
That's a pretty catastrophic decline.
And GoPro has also incurred an operating
loss in every one of every single
quarter over the past two years. And
this is despite the fact that GoPro has
done massive cost cuts and layoffs. So
they've been trying to reduce their
operating expenses, which they have.
However, their revenue and gross profit
is declining even faster. So, they are
continuing to incur operating losses.
So, the fundamental situation for GoPro
does not look very good at all. And now,
let's look at the financial situation uh
their financial position.
So, here in the top left uh corner, this
chart free cash flow uh you can see that
you know throughout 2024 and in the
first quarter of 2025 they had negative
free cash flow. However, in the second
quarter of 2025 and the third quarter of
2025, their free cash flow was actually
slightly positive. Now, the company was
actually losing money during this
period. The only reason the cash flow is
positive is because they've been working
down their networking capital position.
So, the networking capital is inventory
plus accounts receivable plus prepaid
expenses minus accounts payable and
acred expenses. So, what GoPro has done
over the past couple quarters as their
financial situation has gotten
desperate, they've been doing everything
they can to try to work down their
networking capital. So, they've been
running down their inventory. They've
been um trying to extend the payment
terms for their um to their suppliers.
They've been trying to get prepayments
from their retail customers. So from the
retailers that buy GoPro products,
they've been trying to uh get
prepayments from them. They've been
doing everything to possible to improve
their cash position and that is
manifested in the reduction in their
networking capital position. But that of
course will only work for so long.
Eventually you run out of networking
capital and you can no longer play these
games. Now if we look at the cash
position so kind of throughout late 2024
early 2025 GoPros's cash position was
declining as the company you know
incurred operating losses. There was a
big increase in cash in the third
quarter of 2025. So there are two
reasons for this. First of all it's
because they've been working down the
because they've been working down their
networking capital that has resulted in
a cash inflow a temporary cash inflow to
the business. Additionally, they took
out a $30 million or sorry, they took
out a $50 million loan in the third
quarter as a revolving credit facility.
So, they had some, you know, positive
cash flow plus they took out a $50
million loan. That's why their cash
balance increased from $60 million to
about $150 million.
But yeah, we can see a corresponding
increase in their debt um in their
amount of debt that they have because of
that loan, that $50 million loan that
they that they just borrowed in the
third quarter.
Now, this shows if we look here at the
at the top left, the top left chart
shows their free cash flow, but this
free cash flow number is very noisy uh
because it it is impacted by changes in
networking capital. Here I've taken
their free cash flow, but I've stripped
out all of the changes in networking
capital so we can see how much the core
business is actually how much money the
core business is actually losing. And
excluding changes in networking capital,
GoPro burned would have burned $91
million over the trailing 12-month
period. So GoPro's current burn rate is
approximately $90 million per year.
So, as far as their current financial
position, as of September 30th, 2025,
they had $153 million of cash on hand,
but they had a large loan that came due
in November, and that was $94 million.
So, they paid off this, this is
subsequent to the end of the third
quarter, but in November, they paid off
this $94 million loan that came due. So
that brought their cash balance down to
just $60 million. They also have $100
million outstanding in two revolving
credit facilities which are expiring in
2027 and 2028. So they got more than a
year um they got more than a year to
figure out what to do with that. But
they have just $60 million of cash on
hand. They're losing money. They already
have hund00 million of outstanding debt.
So given that they already have so much
debt, I doubt that they'll be able to
borrow much more and they burn they're
the core business is burning
approximately $90 million per year. They
only have $60 million of cash. So, I
think it is very likely that we will see
GoPro conduct dilutive equity issuance
in 2026, especially given that their
stock price is trading at an elevated
level due to the recent meme rally. So,
that gives a perfect opportunity. I'm
kind of s I'm honestly surprised they
have not done any share sales already,
but given their, you know, large amount
of debt that they have plus the fact
that they're burning money and are
probably going to run out of money
within the next few quarters, I think it
is extremely likely that we are going to
see a large, you know, at the at the
market equity offering within the next
few months, which will put downward
pressure on the stock price. So, that
about wraps it up for everything we had
to talk about GoPro today. As always, if
you have any opinions or thoughts about
the company, please let us know in the
comments section below. And we will see
you all on the next video.
